text: The robotic system is precisely docked using laser-guided optics. Ports are placed, and dissection begins below the inferior mesenteric artery using monopolar diathermy. The surgeon carefully dissects behind the superior rectal artery to protect the hypogastric nerve. A window is created at the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, safeguarding the hypogastric nerve and left ureter, facilitating ligation and division of the artery. Hemolog clips secure the artery before division with scissors.

The robotic X-eye system is precisely docked using a laser-guided crossbow for optics. Ports are placed, and dissection begins below the inferior mesenteric artery using monopolar diathermy. The surgeon carefully dissects behind the superior rectal artery to avoid hypogastric nerve injury. The inferior mesenteric artery is isolated at its origin, and a window is created to protect the hypogastric nerve and left ureter. Hemolog clips secure the artery before it is divided with scissors.

During the abdominal perineal dissection, the surgeon divides the femoral ventricular vein close to the artery. Despite the patient's slim physique, the embryological plane in front of Gerota's fascia is visible, highlighted by a thin white line. Precise dissection is performed using paint-like movements with a monopolar diathermy hook or scissors.

During the abdominal perineal dissection, the surgeon divides the femoral ventricular vein close to the artery. Despite the patient's slim physique, the embryological plane is maintained in front of Gerota's fascia, visible as a thin white line. Precise dissection is performed using paint-like movements with a monopolar diathermy hook or scissors.

The surgeon performs pelvic dissection using robotic angulated instruments, staying between the hypogastric nerves. The posterior dissection is followed by division of the peritoneum at the Douglas pouch, with careful protection of the vaginal back wall.

The surgeon performs pelvic dissection following left colonic mobilization, using angulated robotic instruments to navigate between the hypogastric nerves. The posterior dissection is shown, with the peritoneum being divided at the level of the Douglas pouch while protecting the back wall of the vagina.

The surgeon performs precise dissection using monopolar diathermic scissors with gentle traction to facilitate posterior dissection. As the pelvic floor is approached, the specimen is mobilized from the left pelvic side wall. Dissection is carried out anteriorly near the back wall of the vagina, where tethering is observed. The left pelvic side wall is carefully freed, with attention to the left ureter and hypogastric nerve, visible in the high-definition view, to avoid pulling them with excessive friction.

The surgeon uses monopolar diathermic scissors for precise dissection with gentle traction, facilitating posterior dissection. As the pelvic floor is approached, the specimen is mobilized from the left pelvic side wall. Anterior dissection is performed close to the back wall of the vagina, where tethering is observed despite preoperative imaging suggesting a clear vaginal wall. The left pelvic side wall is carefully freed.

The surgeon carefully identifies the left ureter and left hypogastric nerve, ensuring they are not inadvertently pulled during dissection. The angulated instrument provides a high-definition view, aiding in precise dissection near the tethered back wall of the vagina. The dissection progresses safely along the left pelvic side wall, revealing the pelvic floor.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tethered back wall of the vagina, progressing safely to avoid proximity to the tumor. The pelvic side wall on the left reveals the pelvic floor, with the back wall of the vagina visible anteriorly. A small piece of the vaginal backbone is removed and blocked with the specimen to ensure an R-zero dissection, avoiding a positive margin.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection with the back wall of the vagina visible, removing a small piece of vaginal tissue to achieve R-zero resection. Dissection progresses on both sides, revealing the endopelvic fascia and pelvic floor. A gentle digital examination facilitates safe dissection, leaving a small disc of vaginal tissue intact. The dissection continues in a clear, bloodless plane, and a swab is inserted into a small hole in the vagina to prevent gas leakage and maintain pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon progresses the dissection forward on both the right and left sides, revealing the endopelvic fascia and pelvic floor. A gentle digital examination of the back wall of the vagina facilitates the safe dissection, leaving a small disc of the vagina intact. A clear, bloodless plane is visualized as the dissection continues. A swab is inserted into a small hole in the vagina to maintain pneumoperitoneum. The camera, angled 30 degrees upward, provides a static view of the pelvic floor muscles. The modified abdominal perineal resection is performed, sacrificing minimal pelvic floor muscle to achieve R-zero dissection.

The camera is positioned at a thirty-degree upward angle, providing a static view of the pelvic floor muscles. The surgeon performs a modified abdominal perineal resection, sacrificing minimal pelvic floor muscle to achieve an R-zero dissection. The use of a stable camera view and angulated instruments facilitates precise dissection. The intersphincteric plane is entered posteriorly, confirmed by digital examination both luminally and externally on the skin.

The surgeon enters the intersphincteric plane posteriorly, confirmed by digital examination both luminally and externally on the skin. The specimen is mobilized further by cauterizing through to the skin extraluminally, completing the posterior dissection. Similar steps are performed anteriorly, with the left pelvic side wall and intersphincteric plane visible. Gentle traction and counter traction are applied for precision surgery, providing clear anatomical views. Circumferential dissection is carried out on both sides into the intersphincteric groove and onto the skin edge using robotic assistance. A small defect in the vagina is closed with two interrupted POPDS stitches.

The surgeon completes posterior dissection onto the patient's skin using electrocautery, followed by anterior dissection at the left pelvic side wall. The intersphincteric plane is visible, with gentle traction and counter traction providing clear anatomical views. Circumferential dissection is performed on both sides to access the intersphincteric groove and skin edge using robotic instruments. A small defect in the vagina is closed with two interrupted POPDS stitches, and the remaining posterior mobilization involves a small cuff of skin evacuated from below.

The surgeon mobilizes a small cuff of skin posteriorly from below to evacuate the specimen. The blood supply of the left colon is divided proximally using a bipolar device to fashion a left ventricular cyclostomy. The specimen is extracted from the perineum end, revealing a completely excised adenocarcinoma with clear margins.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to divide the blood supply of the left colon proximally, preparing for the creation of a left ventricular cyclostomy. The specimen is extracted from the perineum end, and the excised adenocarcinoma shows clear margins upon examination.
